Home»Sport»Paris Olympics 2024»Prudence Sekgodiso takes positives from first Olympics final

Team South Africa’s Prudence Sekgodiso showed she belongs at the top table of women’s 800m running, despite finishing last in the final.

On a night when Swedish pole vaulter superstarArmand Duplantissoared to previously unscaled heights, Team SA’sPrudence Sekgodisoshowed she belongs at the top table of women’s 800m running.

Enjoy the Paris Olympic Games 2024 experience at your favourite ATKV Resort!

It was a wild programme in front of another 80 000 crowd, something which has been a feature of these 2024 Paris Olympics, with Duplantis leaping 6.25m into the night sky with the final act of the evening.

Beatrice Chebetearlier won the women’s 5 000m over Kenyan team-mate Faith Kipyegon.

Earlier in the evening, Team SA’s men’s 200m hopesWayde van NiekerkandShaun Maswanganiboth finished third in their respective heats toqualify automatically for the semi-finals, butBenjamin Richardsonpulled up with 80m left clutching his upper right leg. He managed to finish the race and was actually credited with a time of 51.26.

This means that if it was only cramp, he can still run in the repechage and reach the semi-finals, while Team SA will behoping he can recoverto be part of the 4x100m relay.

For the latest Paris Olympics 2024 news, bookmarkThe South African website’sdedicated section for free-to-read content

However, the main talking point from a South African perspective was Sekgodiso, who many thought had a chance of earning Team SA their fifth medal at these Games.

It wasn’t to be though and she came over the line in last place after visibly tiring in the last 50 metres.

The 800m gold was won by pre-race favouriteKeely Hodgkinsonin 1:56.72, with Sekgodiso eighth in 1:58.79.

It wasn’t as tight as the men’s 100m final, but two seconds covering eight runners over two laps is an indication of a close race.

Sekgodiso certainly did enough at her first Olympics to confirm she belongs at this level and there’s much to be excited about this 22-year-old as she builds towards the 2028 Los Angeles Games.

She was forced a bit wide early on as the runners jostled for position and she sat alongside Ethiopia’s Tsige Duguma in fourth spot, while Hodgkinson led Kenyan Mary Moraa through the bell in 58.4 seconds.

The speed was injected down the back straight and Sekgodiso went with the leaders.

However, she was kept wide around the far turn, although still had a puncher’s chance entering the final straight. That’s when the race started to tell and she slipped back through the field, knowing her chances had evaporated.

“The plan was to stick with the girls for as long as I could. I know I’m not good in the final 100m and when I saw the girls coming past me, I thought I must just keep my head up and keep going.

“I’m so happy I made the final, c’mon guys, it means a lot to me,” she told the media afterwards.

She also revealed that she has trouble sleeping after a race.

“This was a tough tournament, with three races in three day. I’m not used to that. Normally I can have a day or two to recover but here I couldn’t. I have to take sleeping pills to sleep and then I wake up late, so the recovery period was difficult for a programme like this.”

Sekgodiso will have learned a lot about racing and recovery at these Olympics. And the lessons will stand her in good stead going forward, because she is going to be force to be reckoned with at international level.

Gary Lemkeis reporting live from Paris for the Team SA website for the duration of the Paris Olympics 2024

author: Garrin Lambley
description: Team South Africa’s Prudence Sekgodiso showed she belongs at the top table of women’s 800m running, despite finishing last in the final.
